chunk_summary.idea_trajectory_justification: Participants continuously introduce new concepts—such as adaptation to fluid environments, microbial death, refugia, and water activity in brines—expanding the scope of the discussion rather than narrowing it down.
chunk_summary.collective_engagement_justification: Non-speaking participants frequently nod, smile, and provide audible backchannels, particularly during the discussion on water activity.
chunk_summary.cross_disciplinary_bridging_description: Connected early Earth fossil record of eukaryotes to exoplanet water worlds.
chunk_summary.problem_specificity_justification: The group narrowed the domain to specific extreme conditions (refugia, brines, water activity limits) but has not yet articulated a specific research question.
chunk_summary.decision_crystallization_justification: The conversation remains exploratory, with participants sharing diverse interests without attempting to select a single project direction.
chunk_summary.skill_gap_description: Jennifer Glass identified a need for a chemist to properly define water activity.
chunk_summary.notable_observation: The group showed high engagement and shared humor when discussing the difficulty of defining water activity, indicating a comfortable relational climate and willingness to admit knowledge gaps.

ideas_currently_on_table: Extreme tidal heating and volcanism limiting habitability
Adaptation to fluid environments on water worlds
Microbial death and refugia in extreme environments
Water activity and brines as limits for life
chunk_summaries: The group began with a structured round of introductions where each participant shared their disciplinary background and research focus. Daniel and Anat then guided the group to assign a note-taker and spend time silently reading the prompt. Following the reading period, Nicolas and Laura proposed initial research ideas regarding the habitability of extreme water worlds and the effects of extreme tidal heating and chemical diversity, shifting the discussion into early divergent exploration.

The group engaged in divergent exploration of extreme environments and habitability limits. Laura Schaefer discussed the impacts of extreme volcanism, while Simon Darroch and Kimberly Lau explored how organisms adapt to fluid environments and spatial gradients. Trinity Hamilton and Morgan Raven introduced the concept of microbial death and refugia, leading Jennifer Glass to propose brines and water activity as critical limits for life, which sparked a collaborative effort to define water activity.
